title: Project setup: ROS2 package scaffold and launch infrastructure
body:
## Description
Create the foundational ROS2 package structure that all nodes will build on. This includes the package manifest, launch files, parameter configs, and a shared constants/state-machine module used by all three nodes.

## Technical suggestions
- Use `ros2 pkg create` to scaffold the package with `ament_python`
- Create a top-level `launch/` directory with a `snc.launch.py` that starts all three nodes, SLAM Toolbox, Nav2, and `find_object_2d` together
- Define a `config/params.yaml` for tunable parameters:
  - `waypoint_spacing_m` — minimum distance between recorded path poses (suggested 0.15 m)
  - `marker_confidence_threshold` — minimum `find_object_2d` score to accept a detection
  - `spin_duration_s` — how long Node 1 pauses and spins when a marker signal is received
  - `pose_sample_interval_s` — Node 3 path-recording timer period
- Create a `snc/constants.py` shared module containing:
  - Hazard marker ID mapping (spec table, IDs 0–12)
  - Start marker object ID (to be excluded from `/hazards`)
  - Internal topic name constants (`SNC_HAZARD_SIGNAL`, `SNC_ROBOT_POSE`, `SNC_RETURN_WAYPOINTS`)
  - Status string constants (`STATUS_WAITING`, `STATUS_EXPLORING`, etc.)
- Add a `requirements.txt` / `package.xml` listing all external dependencies: `nav2_*`, `find_object_2d`, `slam_toolbox`, `tf2_ros`, `visualization_msgs`, `nav_msgs`
- Set up a `README.md` documenting how to build, configure, and launch

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Package builds cleanly with `colcon build`
- [ ] `snc.launch.py` launches all nodes, SLAM, Nav2, and `find_object_2d` without errors (even if nodes are stubs)
- [ ] All external package dependencies declared in `package.xml`
- [ ] `config/params.yaml` exists with parameters for all three nodes
- [ ] `snc/constants.py` exists with hazard ID map, topic name constants, and status string constants
- [ ] README documents build and launch steps

title: Node 2: Start marker detection to trigger exploration
body:
## Description
Detect the visual start marker shown to the camera and publish a trigger to begin the Search & Navigation Challenge. This responsibility belongs to Node 2 (hazard detection), since it already processes `find_object_2d` output. Also subscribe to `/trigger_start` as a contingency fallback.

## Technical suggestions
- The start marker has a distinct `find_object_2d` object ID — load it alongside the hazard marker training images
- On detection of the start marker, publish a `std_msgs/Empty` to `/snc_start` (internal) to signal Node 1 to begin exploration
- Subscribe to `/trigger_start` (`std_msgs/Empty`) from the assessor and treat receipt as equivalent to visual detection — publish to `/snc_start` on receipt
- Guard against repeated triggers — set a boolean flag so the start signal fires only once
- The start marker ID must be stored in `snc/constants.py` and must **never** be published to `/hazards`
- Update `/snc_status` with `STATUS_WAITING` → `STATUS_EXPLORING` on trigger

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Robot begins exploration when start marker is held in front of the camera
- [ ] Robot begins exploration when `ros2 topic pub /trigger_start std_msgs/msg/Empty '{}'` is run
- [ ] `/snc_status` reflects the state transition from waiting to exploring
- [ ] Start marker ID is never published to `/hazards`
- [ ] No duplicate triggers cause erratic behaviour (flag guard in place)

title: Node 1: Autonomous exploration strategy
body:
## Description
Implement the core autonomous exploration algorithm that drives the ROSBot around the unknown closed space until all hazard markers are found or the 4-minute timeout elapses.

## Technical suggestions
- Subscribe to `/snc_start` (`std_msgs/Empty`, published by Node 2) to begin exploration — do not start movement until this signal arrives
- Choose one primary approach and extend it with custom logic beyond the stock algorithm:
  - **Frontier exploration**: parse `/map` (`nav_msgs/OccupancyGrid`) to find frontier cells (known-free cells adjacent to unknown cells), cluster them, select the nearest/largest, and send a `NavigateToPose` Nav2 action goal
  - **Explore Lite**: integrate the `explore_lite` package but add custom spinning and marker-count stopping behaviour on top
  - **Wall following**: adapt the TheConstruct example with Nav2 or direct `/cmd_vel`, adding frontier detection to avoid cycles
- Subscribe to `/snc_hazard_signal` (`std_msgs/Empty`, published by Node 2 on each new confirmed marker) — on receipt, cancel the current Nav2 goal, spin 360° in place (`/cmd_vel`) to maximise camera coverage, then resume exploration
- Monitor the count of unique markers on `/hazards`; stop exploring and trigger Node 3's return-home once 5 unique markers are confirmed
- Implement a 4-minute timeout: if 5 markers are not found, publish to `/snc_return_trigger` (internal) to begin return-home anyway
- Subscribe to `/trigger_teleop` (`std_msgs/Empty`) — on receipt, cancel all Nav2 goals and suspend autonomous movement so a teleop node can take over; update status to `STATUS_TELEOP`
- Publish `/snc_status` at ~1 Hz with current state string throughout

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Robot does not move until `/snc_start` or `/trigger_start` is received
- [ ] Robot explores the space without collisions (Nav2 obstacle avoidance active)
- [ ] Robot stops and spins on each new `/snc_hazard_signal` received
- [ ] Exploration terminates automatically when 5 unique markers are confirmed on `/hazards`
- [ ] Exploration terminates after 4-minute timeout and hands off to return-home
- [ ] `/trigger_teleop` suspends autonomous movement cleanly
- [ ] Custom logic beyond a stock algorithm is demonstrable (spin behaviour, frontier filtering, or marker-count stopping)

title: Node 2: Hazard marker detection via find_object_2d
body:
## Description
Integrate `find_object_2d` to detect hazard markers (and the start marker) in the camera image and identify which of the 13 defined marker types has been seen.

## Technical suggestions
- Launch `find_object_2d` with the full hazard marker image set from Canvas **plus** the start marker image as training data; all images must be loaded
- Subscribe to `find_object_2d`'s output topic (typically `/objects`) which provides object ID and 2D bounding-box homography
- Map `find_object_2d` object IDs to the SNC hazard marker IDs (0–12) defined in the spec using the mapping in `snc/constants.py`; the start marker ID must also be in `constants.py` and routed to the start trigger, not to `/hazards`
- Apply a confidence/size threshold (configurable via `config/params.yaml`) to reject false positives — reject detections where the homography bounding box is very small (likely a distant false positive)
- Publish validated detection events to an internal topic for Node 2's localisation pipeline
- Debounce: do not re-trigger localisation for a marker ID that has already been successfully localised and published to `/hazards`

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] All 13 hazard marker types plus the start marker loaded as training images into `find_object_2d`
- [ ] Correct hazard marker ID (per spec table, from `constants.py`) is used for each detection
- [ ] Confidence/size threshold is configurable via `params.yaml` and is active
- [ ] Already-localised markers are not repeatedly re-processed
- [ ] Start marker detection routes to start trigger, not to `/hazards`

title: Node 2: Hazard marker localisation using secondary sensor
body:
## Description
Compute the map-frame position of each detected hazard marker using a secondary sensor (depth image, laser, or IR range) combined with the TF transform tree, and publish to `/hazards`.

## Technical suggestions
- **Recommended — depth image approach**: when a marker detection event arrives, extract the pixel coordinates of the bounding-box centre from the homography; look up the depth at that pixel from `/camera/depth/image_raw`; back-project to a 3D point using camera intrinsics; transform from `camera_frame` → `map` via `tf2_ros.Buffer.lookup_transform`
- **Alternative — laser approach**: map the bounding-box centre column to a horizontal angle; find the closest laser beam in `/scan` at that angle; transform the range reading from `laser_frame` → `map`
- **Alternative — IR range**: use front-facing IR sensors as a distance estimate combined with robot heading from TF
- Accumulate several position readings for each marker (e.g. over 1–2 seconds or N frames) into a buffer; publish the **median** x/y to `/hazards` rather than a single noisy reading — this is the mode-filter approach that prevents inconsistent placement from transient detections
- Construct a `visualization_msgs/Marker` message with:
  - `header.frame_id = 'map'`
  - `id` set to the SNC hazard ID from `constants.py`
  - `type = Marker.SPHERE` or `Marker.CUBE`
  - `pose` set to the computed map-frame position
- After publishing a confirmed marker, publish a `std_msgs/Empty` to `/snc_hazard_signal` to notify Node 1 to pause and spin
- Do not publish the start marker to `/hazards` under any circumstances
- Update `/snc_status` with the detected marker name (e.g. `'marker detected: explosive'`) on each successful localisation

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Marker position in `/hazards` is within ~30 cm of the true position in a controlled test (document actual measured error)
- [ ] `frame_id` is `'map'` on all published markers
- [ ] Marker `id` matches the spec table values from `constants.py`
- [ ] Secondary sensor data (not just camera RGB) is demonstrably used — depth/laser/IR reading is logged or visible in evidence
- [ ] Median/mode position filter over a buffer window is implemented
- [ ] `/snc_hazard_signal` is published to Node 1 after each confirmed marker localisation
- [ ] Start marker is never published to `/hazards`
- [ ] `/snc_status` reflects each new marker detection by name

title: Node 3: Robot path tracking during exploration
body:
## Description
Periodically record the robot's map-frame pose during exploration, expose the pose history to the return-home logic, and publish the accumulated path on `/path_explore`.

## Technical suggestions
- Use `tf2_ros.Buffer.lookup_transform('map', 'base_link', rclpy.time.Time())` on a timer to get the current pose
- Use a **distance-and-angle threshold** to decide whether to record each sample — only append a new pose if the robot has moved ≥ `waypoint_spacing_m` (from `params.yaml`, suggested 0.15 m) OR rotated ≥ 15° since the last recorded pose. This keeps path density suitable for Nav2 waypoint following without gaps.
- Store poses as a `list[PoseStamped]` accessible to the return-home logic within the same node
- Publish the full accumulated list as `nav_msgs/Path` on `/path_explore` with `header.frame_id = 'map'` after each new pose is appended
- Begin recording immediately when Node 3 starts; stop recording when the return-home phase is triggered (via internal flag or `/snc_return_trigger`)

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] `/path_explore` updates continuously during exploration in the `map` frame
- [ ] Path density is controlled by distance/angle threshold — no large gaps (> 0.5 m) and no excessive density (< 0.1 m between consecutive points)
- [ ] Recording stops cleanly when return-home is triggered
- [ ] Pose list is accessible in-memory to the return-home logic in the same node

title: Node 3: Return-to-home by retracing exploration path
body:
## Description
On completion of exploration (automatic via marker count / timeout, or manual via `/trigger_home`), navigate the robot back to its start position by following the recorded exploration path in reverse order.

## Technical suggestions
- Subscribe to `/trigger_home` (`std_msgs/Empty`) for manual invocation; also listen to an internal flag set by Node 1's timeout/completion logic
- Reverse the stored `list[PoseStamped]` from the path tracker
- **Invert quaternions** for each pose in the reversed list so the robot faces the direction of travel during return, not backwards — negate the `z` and `w` components of each quaternion (or compute the heading from consecutive waypoint positions)
- Thin the reversed waypoint list if needed: if consecutive points are < `waypoint_spacing_m` apart, skip intermediates to prevent Nav2 goal thrashing
- Choose a return approach:
  - **Nav2 `FollowWaypoints` action** (`nav2_msgs/action/FollowWaypoints`): send the full reversed + thinned list in one action call
  - **Sequential `NavigateToPose` goals**: iterate through reversed waypoints, send one goal at a time, await completion before sending the next
  - **Direct `/cmd_vel` PID**: compute heading error and distance to each waypoint; drive with proportional control
- Stop and publish zero velocity once the start position is within an acceptable radius (e.g. 0.3 m); hold for ≥ 5 seconds before marking challenge complete
- Update `/snc_status` to `STATUS_RETURNING` at the start and `STATUS_COMPLETE` on arrival

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Robot returns to start by following the approximate reverse of the exploration path — a direct straight-line shortcut is not acceptable
- [ ] Quaternions are inverted/corrected so the robot faces forward along the return path
- [ ] `/trigger_home` manually triggers return when the automatic trigger has not fired
- [ ] Robot stops and holds position for ≥ 5 s at the home location
- [ ] Return begins within a few seconds of exploration ending
- [ ] `/snc_status` transitions through `STATUS_RETURNING` and `STATUS_COMPLETE`

title: Node 3: Publish return-home path on /path_return
body:
## Description
Track and publish the robot's path during the return-to-home phase on the separate `/path_return` topic, reusing the same TF-based sampling logic from the exploration path tracker.

## Technical suggestions
- Begin recording to a **second** `list[PoseStamped]` when return-home is triggered; do not write into the exploration path list
- Use the same distance/angle threshold sampling logic as the exploration path tracker (share the helper function within the node)
- Publish as `nav_msgs/Path` on `/path_return` with `header.frame_id = 'map'`, updating after each new pose is appended
- Stop recording when the robot halts at the home position
- Both `/path_explore` and `/path_return` should be simultaneously visible in RViz/Foxglove during the return phase — verify this in testing

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] `/path_return` is published and updates throughout the return phase in the `map` frame
- [ ] `/path_explore` and `/path_return` are distinct lists — return recording does not overwrite the explore path
- [ ] Both paths are simultaneously visible in RViz/Foxglove
- [ ] Recording stops when the robot halts at home

title: SLAM and map publishing setup
body:
## Description
Configure and launch SLAM Toolbox to build and publish an occupancy grid map on `/map` throughout the challenge, and verify the TF tree is complete end-to-end.

## Technical suggestions
- Use `slam_toolbox` in online async mode: `slam_toolbox/launch/online_async_launch.py`
- Provide a tuned `config/mapper_params_online_async.yaml` (adjust resolution, scan matching params for the ROSBot's laser)
- Verify the TF tree is complete with no gaps: `map` → `odom` → `base_link` → `laser_frame` → `camera_frame` — all frames needed by Node 2's localisation and Node 3's path tracker must be present
- Test map quality in simulation before deploying on hardware
- Include SLAM launch in `snc.launch.py`

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] `/map` is published and updates in real time during robot movement
- [ ] Map accurately reflects walls and obstacles in a test environment
- [ ] TF tree has no gaps or extrapolation errors — verify with `ros2 run tf2_tools view_frames`
- [ ] All sensor frames (`laser_frame`, `camera_frame`) are present in the TF tree
- [ ] SLAM launches as part of `snc.launch.py`

title: Nav2 stack configuration and bringup
body:
## Description
Configure and integrate Nav2 for obstacle-aware point-to-point navigation used by Node 1 (exploration goals) and Node 3 (return-home waypoints).

## Technical suggestions
- Use `nav2_bringup` with a custom `config/nav2_params.yaml` tuned for the ROSBot (footprint, costmap inflation radius, planner and controller speeds)
- Configure both global and local costmaps to use `/map` and `/scan`
- Confirm `NavigateToPose` and `FollowWaypoints` action servers are available and reachable from Python action clients in Node 1 and Node 3
- Test recovery behaviours (spin, back-up) so the robot can self-recover from getting stuck
- Ensure Nav2 respects the `map` → `odom` → `base_link` TF chain provided by SLAM Toolbox
- Include Nav2 launch in `snc.launch.py`

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Robot navigates to a specified `NavigateToPose` goal without hitting obstacles in a test run
- [ ] `FollowWaypoints` action server is reachable and tested with a short waypoint list
- [ ] Recovery behaviours activate and recover the robot when it gets stuck
- [ ] Nav2 launches cleanly as part of `snc.launch.py` alongside SLAM

title: Shared status topic implementation (/snc_status)
body:
## Description
Implement a shared status publisher so the robot's current operational state is always visible on `/snc_status` throughout the challenge. All three nodes publish to this topic using a common state string set defined in `snc/constants.py`.

## Technical suggestions
- Define status string constants in `snc/constants.py`, e.g.:
  - `STATUS_WAITING = 'waiting for start'`
  - `STATUS_EXPLORING = 'exploring'`
  - `STATUS_MARKER_DETECTED = 'marker detected: {name}'` (Node 2 fills in the marker name)
  - `STATUS_RETURNING = 'returning home'`
  - `STATUS_COMPLETE = 'challenge complete'`
  - `STATUS_TELEOP = 'teleop active'`
- Each node imports constants and publishes `std_msgs/String` to `/snc_status` at ~1 Hz during the phase it owns
- Whichever node currently owns the active phase is responsible for publishing; other nodes may publish on transitions
- Ensure status is visible in Foxglove/RViz during the challenge demo

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] `/snc_status` publishes continuously throughout all phases of the challenge
- [ ] Status string changes meaningfully at each phase transition (waiting → exploring → returning → complete)
- [ ] Marker detections are reflected in status messages with the marker name
- [ ] `STATUS_TELEOP` is published when `/trigger_teleop` is received
- [ ] Status strings come from `constants.py` — no magic strings scattered across nodes

title: Integration testing in simulation
body:
## Description
Run the full software stack end-to-end in a simulated environment (TheConstruct or Gazebo) and verify all required topics publish correct, valid data before moving to the real robot.

## Technical suggestions
- Set up a Gazebo world with walls, obstacles, and printed hazard marker textures on the walls
- Record a ROS2 bag (`ros2 bag record -a`) covering all required topics: `/map`, `/hazards`, `/path_explore`, `/path_return`, `/snc_status` plus the inter-node topics `/snc_hazard_signal`, `/snc_robot_pose`
- After the run, inspect the bag in RViz: verify marker `id` values, `frame_id = 'map'` on all published markers and paths, and approximate coordinate accuracy
- Verify Node 1 pauses and spins each time `/snc_hazard_signal` is received
- Verify Node 3 path reversal and quaternion inversion produce sensible return poses
- Document any sim-to-real discrepancies (laser noise profile, camera exposure, depth image range) for tuning on hardware

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Full challenge completes in simulation without manual intervention
- [ ] All 5 required output topics (`/map`, `/hazards`, `/path_explore`, `/path_return`, `/snc_status`) publish valid data throughout the run
- [ ] All inter-node topics (`/snc_hazard_signal`, `/snc_robot_pose`, `/snc_return_waypoints`) are active and correct
- [ ] ROS2 bag recorded for at least one complete simulated run and reviewed in RViz
- [ ] Known sim-to-real gaps documented in the repo wiki

title: Real-robot testing and parameter tuning
body:
## Description
Deploy and test the full software stack on the physical ROSBot in the AIIL/Cyber Security Lab and tune parameters for real-world performance.

## Technical suggestions
- Start with a small, known environment before using the unknown challenge space
- Tune SLAM resolution and Nav2 costmap inflation for the physical laser's noise characteristics
- Adjust `marker_confidence_threshold` in `params.yaml` for real lighting conditions in the lab
- Measure actual hazard marker placement error: record the published `/hazards` position and physically measure the true marker position; document the delta
- Test and tune `waypoint_spacing_m` for return-home: too small → Nav2 goal thrashing; too large → path not faithfully retraced
- Verify TF accuracy end-to-end: confirm depth/laser transform into map frame produces positions close to physical marker locations
- Charge ROSBots after each session; log any hardware issues on Teams/BitBucket wiki immediately

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Full challenge attempted on the real robot at least twice before demo day
- [ ] Hazard marker placement error documented with measured vs. published position (in cm)
- [ ] Return-to-home path visually follows the exploration path on the real robot (visible in RViz recording)
- [ ] `waypoint_spacing_m` tuned and final value recorded in `params.yaml` with rationale in README
- [ ] Hardware issues logged and reported on Teams/BitBucket

title: Evaluation report: collect evidence throughout development
body:
## Description
Systematically collect video and data evidence throughout the project to support the evaluation report. Collect as you go — do not leave this until the end.

## Technical suggestions
- Record short screen-capture videos of RViz/Foxglove showing: map building in real time, marker detection with marker IDs and names visible on `/snc_status`, `/path_explore` overlaid on the map, return-home trajectory overlaid as `/path_return`
- For each real-robot test run, save a ROS2 bag file (`ros2 bag record /map /hazards /path_explore /path_return /snc_status`)
- Keep a running log of limitations encountered (e.g. false positives under certain lighting, Nav2 goal failures) and how they were addressed
- Screenshot RViz showing both `/path_explore` and `/path_return` simultaneously during a return-home run
- Photograph or video the physical marker placement next to the RViz-displayed position to evidence localisation accuracy
- Track AI tool usage in real time — export Claude/ChatGPT logs as you go, do not reconstruct from memory
- Commit an `evidence/` folder to the repo with a brief `index.md` listing each file and what it demonstrates

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] At least 3 video recordings of real-robot test runs captured before demo day
- [ ] ROS2 bag file from at least one successful near-complete run saved and committed or linked
- [ ] AI tool usage log maintained and exportable
- [ ] Localisation error measured and documented with video/photo evidence
- [ ] `evidence/index.md` committed to the repo

title: Evaluation report: write and submit PDF
body:
## Description
Compile all evidence and analysis into a ≤5-page PDF evaluation report, due 11:59 pm Sunday 19 April 2026.

## Technical suggestions
- Suggested structure (fit within 5 pages):
  1. **Node summaries**: one paragraph per node describing its algorithm and the custom logic beyond any stock package
  2. **Package overview**: list all ROS2 dependent packages (`slam_toolbox`, `nav2_*`, `find_object_2d`, `tf2_ros`, etc.), describe `snc.launch.py`, `params.yaml`, `constants.py`, and any `find_object_2d` training data
  3. **Performance analysis**: results from demo + independent testing; include measured hazard marker placement error, path-following fidelity, and completion time; cite video timestamps or bag file screenshots as evidence
  4. **Strengths**: specific, evidenced claims (e.g. "marker localisation achieved < 20 cm error in 4/5 tests — see video at 2:14")
  5. **Limitations**: specific failure modes observed and their root causes
  6. **References + AI log summary**: cite all ROS2 packages, papers, and tutorials; summarise AI tool use with reference to the exported log
- All claims must be backed by specific evidence (video timestamp, bag file screenshot, measured numbers) — generalisations will not receive marks
- Upload accompanying videos to the MS Teams location specified on Canvas **before** the deadline
- Silence period begins 5:00 pm Friday 17 April — submit all questions before then

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] PDF is ≤ 5 pages
- [ ] Every performance claim is supported by referenced, specific evidence
- [ ] All dependent ROS2 packages listed with version/source
- [ ] Launch files, `params.yaml`, `constants.py`, and `find_object_2d` training data described
- [ ] AI tool usage log included or summarised with reference to the exported file
- [ ] Accompanying videos uploaded to MS Teams before the deadline
- [ ] PDF submitted via Canvas before 11:59 pm 19 April 2026
